questions.prompt: What is in-context learning actually doing when you add few-shot examples to a prompt?
options: Updating the model's weights with your examples
Conditioning the model to select a pattern it already learned during training
Adding new factual knowledge the model didn't have
Fine-tuning a temporary copy of the model for your session
questions.explanation: Examples in the prompt don't train anything — the weights are frozen. They condition generation, steering the model toward patterns and formats it already learned in pre-training. That's why examples are great for demonstrating format and policy but can't inject new knowledge.

questions.prompt: What should you do before investing in few-shot examples for a task?
options: Write at least ten examples so the model has enough data
Switch to a base (non-instruction-tuned) model first
Baseline the task zero-shot — modern instruction-tuned models often don't need examples
Raise the temperature so the model explores more formats
questions.explanation: Modern instruction-tuned models handle many tasks well from instructions alone. Always measure zero-shot performance first; if it's already sufficient, examples just add token cost. Add examples when you need to demonstrate format, style, or edge-case policy.

questions.prompt: Which practices improve few-shot example quality? (Select all that apply.)
options: Choosing examples that define the boundaries of tricky cases
Including one deliberately wrong example so the model learns what to avoid
Balancing labels so the model doesn't learn a majority-class bias
Encoding examples as alternating user/assistant turns
questions.explanation: Boundary-defining examples, balanced labels, and alternating-turn encoding all measurably help. A wrong example actively hurts — the model imitates what it sees, and ordering/recency effects mean it may copy the error. Never include an incorrect demonstration.

questions.prompt: What is the main trade-off of dynamic few-shot (retrieving similar examples per input)?
options: It only works with base models, not instruction-tuned ones
Quality scales with your example library, but the changing prompt prefix sacrifices prompt-cache hits
It requires fine-tuning the embedding model first
It forces you to use more than 20 examples per request
questions.explanation: Retrieving the most relevant examples per input improves quality as your library grows, but because the examples change per request, the prompt prefix is no longer stable — which defeats prompt caching and raises cost. Static examples keep the prefix cacheable.

questions.prompt: Why does chain-of-thought prompting improve accuracy on hard problems?
options: It activates a separate logic engine inside the model
Reasoning tokens buy the model more serial computation and act as working memory before it commits to an answer
It lowers the effective temperature of generation
Longer outputs are always rated higher by the training process
questions.explanation: Each generated token is a fixed amount of compute. By reasoning first, the model spends many tokens of computation — and can store intermediate results in the visible text — before producing the answer. That's also why the answer must come after the reasoning, never before.

questions.prompt: You're asking a model for JSON output with both a 'reasoning' field and an 'answer' field. What ordering matters?
options: Put 'answer' first so it's easy to parse
Field order is irrelevant since JSON objects are unordered
Put 'reasoning' before 'answer' so the answer is generated after the reasoning tokens
Use two separate API calls; CoT can't be combined with JSON
questions.explanation: Generation is sequential: tokens earlier in the output influence later tokens, not vice versa. If 'answer' is generated first, the reasoning is post-hoc rationalization that couldn't have improved it. Order the schema so reasoning fields precede the answer field.

questions.prompt: How should you adapt prompting for reasoning models with extended thinking?
options: Give them goals and success criteria, and tune the thinking budget, rather than scripting step-by-step instructions
Always add 'think step by step' — it works identically on all model types
Disable thinking entirely since it duplicates your prompt's instructions
Provide more few-shot examples to replace the thinking phase
questions.explanation: Reasoning models learned to deliberate via RL, so micromanaging their steps often hurts. State the goal and the criteria for a good answer, and control depth via the thinking budget. Explicit CoT scripting still pays off on standard, non-reasoning models — which serve most high-volume traffic.

questions.prompt: Which statements about reasoning techniques are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: Self-consistency samples multiple reasoning paths and takes a majority vote, trading cost for reliability
A model's stated chain of thought is a guaranteed faithful account of how it reached its answer
Decomposing a hard task into smaller calls can beat one monolithic prompt
Verification passes work best when checking an answer is easier than producing it
questions.explanation: Self-consistency, decomposition, and verifier passes are all real, cost-for-reliability trades. But chains of thought are not guaranteed faithful — models can produce correct-sounding reasoning that didn't drive the answer, so verify claims mechanically when it matters.

questions.prompt: Why should a system prompt be treated as a software contract?
options: Because providers legally require system prompts to be versioned
Because downstream code depends on the output format and behavioral rules it specifies, so changes can break consumers
Because system prompts are compiled into the model at deploy time
Because users can read the system prompt and hold you to it
questions.explanation: Your application code parses what the model produces and assumes the behavioral rules hold. Changing the system prompt changes that interface — exactly like changing a function signature — so it deserves version control, code review, evals before deploy, and per-request version logging.

questions.prompt: What is an 'escape hatch' in a system prompt?
options: A hidden command that lets developers bypass the model's rules
A defined action for the model to take when it's uncertain or the input is out of scope
A fallback model used when the primary model is overloaded
A stop sequence that truncates overly long outputs
questions.explanation: Without a defined action for uncertainty or off-topic input, the model improvises — usually by guessing confidently. Telling it exactly what to do instead ('if the request is not about billing, respond with...') converts unpredictable failures into designed behavior.

questions.prompt: Which are sound system prompt practices? (Select all that apply.)
options: Phrase rules positively and pair every 'never' with what to do instead
Place untrusted user data inside the system prompt so the model treats it with authority
Structure the prompt predictably: role, context, rules, output format, examples, escape hatches
Enforce hard security requirements in code, not in prompt prose
questions.explanation: Positive, specific instructions outperform bare prohibitions; predictable structure aids maintenance; and prose rules are suggestions, not guarantees — security belongs in code. Untrusted input must never go in the system prompt: that's handing attacker text your highest-trust position (see Module 8).

questions.prompt: You upgrade to a new model version without touching your system prompt. What should happen first?
options: Nothing — the prompt didn't change, so behavior won't either
Treat it as a migration: run your eval suite, because model upgrades shift behavior under an unchanged prompt
Rewrite the prompt from scratch for the new model
Lower the temperature to compensate for the new model
questions.explanation: The system's behavior is a function of prompt AND model. A new model can interpret the same instructions differently — formats drift, edge-case handling changes. Gate the upgrade behind the same eval run you'd require for a prompt edit.

questions.prompt: What does the 'lost in the middle' finding say about long contexts?
options: Models recall the middle of the context best and the edges worst
Recall is U-shaped: strongest at the beginning and end of the context, weakest in the middle
Models ignore everything after the first 4,000 tokens
Long contexts only degrade performance for code, not prose
questions.explanation: Empirically, information at the start and end of a long context is recalled far better than information buried in the middle. Practical consequence: instructions at the top, bulk reference data in the middle, and the actual task — plus restated critical rules — at the end.

questions.prompt: Why does prompt caching reward putting stable content first in your prompt?
options: Caches are keyed on a prompt prefix — one changed byte invalidates everything after it
Models attend more strongly to the start of the prompt
Providers only cache the first 1,000 tokens
Stable content compresses better in transit
questions.explanation: Prompt caching reuses computation for an exact-matching prefix. If a timestamp or user name appears early, every token after it misses the cache on every request. Order stable-first (system prompt, tools, examples), volatile-last, and keep changing values out of the system prompt.

questions.prompt: Why can adding more (loosely relevant) context reduce answer quality even when it fits in the window?
options: The API truncates inputs above 50% of the window
Attention is a finite budget — irrelevant tokens dilute it and distract the model, beyond just costing money
Longer prompts force the temperature higher
Models penalize prompts that exceed an internal word count
questions.explanation: A big window means the model *can* read more, not that it reads everything equally well. Irrelevant material competes for attention with what matters, measurably degrading accuracy. Treat the window as a paid attention budget and curate what goes in.

questions.prompt: Which are valid strategies for a chat history that grows without bound? (Select all that apply.)
options: Truncate to the most recent N turns
Summarize older turns and keep the summary plus recent turns
Rely on the model remembering earlier turns from previous API calls
Externalize durable facts to memory or retrieval and pull them back when relevant
questions.explanation: Truncation, summarization, and externalizing to memory/RAG are the three standard policies — each trading fidelity, cost, and complexity differently. Relying on the model's memory fails structurally: the API is stateless, and the model sees only what you send (Module 1).

questions.prompt: What is the defining mistake of amateur prompt iteration?
options: Using files instead of a prompt-management dashboard
Judging a prompt change by eyeballing one or two outputs instead of re-running a fixed test set
Testing with real user inputs instead of synthetic ones
Changing the prompt more than once per week
questions.explanation: A change that fixes the case in front of you can silently break others. The professional loop runs every change against the same fixed set of cases and compares results — improvement must be measured across the set, not vibed from one output.

questions.prompt: Your prompt's output format keeps drifting despite a detailed format description. Per the debugging table, what's the first fix to try?
options: Raise max_tokens so the model has room to comply
Add a few-shot example demonstrating the exact format
Switch to a larger model
Repeat the format description three times
questions.explanation: Format is communicated far more reliably by demonstration than by description. A single concrete example of the desired output typically fixes drift that paragraphs of prose specification cannot.

questions.prompt: Which practices belong to a disciplined prompt workflow? (Select all that apply.)
options: Change one variable at a time so you know which edit moved the score
Keep prompts in version-controlled files and log the version with every request
Re-run the full test set when upgrading the model, even if the prompt is unchanged
Skip re-testing when a change only adds a new rule, since additions can't cause regressions
questions.explanation: Single-variable changes, versioned prompt files with per-request logging, and treating model upgrades as changes are all core discipline. Additions absolutely can regress behavior — a new rule can contradict an old one or shift attention — so everything gets re-tested.

questions.prompt: After several disciplined iterations, accuracy on factual questions has plateaued and the model still invents details. What's the right move?
options: Keep iterating — every task is solvable with enough prompt refinement
Add 'do not hallucinate' to the system prompt in capital letters
Recognize prompting's limit: missing knowledge needs retrieval (RAG), not more prose
Increase the temperature to encourage more diverse answers
questions.explanation: Prompting selects behavior the model already has; it cannot add knowledge the model lacks. When failures are missing or stale facts, the fix is retrieval (Module 4) — just as computation failures want tool calling (Module 3) and stubborn style issues may want fine-tuning (Module 6).
